name: PEDS Exam 2 — Chapters 20-24: Adolescence, Hospitalization, Neuro/Sensory & Musculoskeletal

questions.question: A 15-year-old is questioning their values and experimenting with different roles. According to Erikson, which stage is this?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Identity vs Role Confusion (12–18 years) involves questioning values, experimenting with roles, and developing a sense of self. A is incorrect — Industry vs Inferiority is school-age (6–12 years). C is incorrect — Intimacy vs Isolation is young adult (18–40 years). D is incorrect — Generativity vs Stagnation is middle adult (40–65 years).

questions.question: Which statements about Tanner stages are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Tanner stage 1 is prepubertal (no secondary sex characteristics).
Tanner stage 5 represents full sexual maturity.
Breast development in girls begins before pubic hair.
Boys develop pubic hair before testicular growth.
Tanner stages are used to assess pubertal development.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, E): A is correct — Tanner 1 is prepubertal. B is correct — Tanner 5 is mature adult. C is correct — breast development precedes pubic hair in girls. E is correct — Tanner stages assess pubertal development. D is incorrect — testicular growth precedes pubic hair in boys.

questions.question: The HEADSS assessment is used to screen adolescents. What does the 'H' stand for?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): HEADSS is a psychosocial screening tool for adolescents: Home, Education/Employment, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/Depression. A is incorrect — H is Home, not Health. C is incorrect — Activities (not Hobbies). D is incorrect — not Hormones.

questions.question: What is the leading cause of death in adolescents?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Motor vehicle accidents (MVA) are the leading cause of death in adolescents (ages 13–19). Risk factors include inexperience, distraction (phones), speeding, and not wearing seat belts. A is incorrect — cancer is significant but not leading. C is incorrect — suicide is second leading. D is incorrect — infections are lower due to immunity/vaccines.

questions.question: Which vaccines are recommended for adolescents? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, E): A is correct — MenACWY at 11-12 and 16 years. B is correct — HPV at 11-12 years (2-3 doses). C is correct — Tdap at 11-12 years. E is correct — annual flu. D is incorrect — MMR is given at 12 months and 4-6 years; booster not routine in adolescence.

questions.question: A 16-year-old reports feeling sad, hopeless, and withdrawing from friends. What is the nurse's priority action?
options: Tell the teen to cheer up and be more social
Screen for suicidal ideation and notify provider
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Screen for suicidal ideation and notify the healthcare provider. Depression in adolescents is serious — suicide is the second leading cause of death in this age group. A is incorrect — dismissive and dangerous. C is incorrect — nurses don't prescribe. D is incorrect — not the priority action.

questions.question: Match each adolescent developmental task with the correct description:
rows: Developing abstract thinking and future planning
Experimenting with different roles and values
Contributing to society and guiding others
questions.rationale: Correct: Formal Operational (abstract thinking) → A. Identity vs Role Confusion (role experimentation) → B. Intimacy vs Isolation (intimate relationships) → C. Generativity vs Stagnation (contributing to society) → D. These are the correct Piaget and Erikson stages for adolescents and adults.

questions.question: A 6-year-old hospitalized child becomes withdrawn and quiet after parents leave. This is which stage of separation anxiety?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Despair is the second stage of separation anxiety. The child becomes quiet, withdrawn, depressed, and may refuse to eat or play. A is incorrect — protest is loud crying and resistance. C is incorrect — detachment appears as 'adjustment' but is emotional withdrawal. D is incorrect — not a standard stage.

questions.question: Which pain assessment tools are appropriate for children? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — FLACC for nonverbal infants/children. B is correct — FACES for children 3+ years. C is correct — numeric for school-age who understand numbers. D is correct — CRIES for neonates. E is incorrect — visual analog is for school-age+.

questions.question: A child with suspected meningitis is being evaluated. Which signs are positive for Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs?
options: Kernig: knee flexion causes pain; Brudzinski: neck flexion causes hip flexion
Kernig: hip flexion causes knee pain; Brudzinski: hip extension causes neck pain
Kernig: neck flexion causes leg extension; Brudzinski: knee flexion causes neck pain
Kernig and Brudzinski are not used in children
questions.rationale: Correct (A): Kernig's sign: with patient supine, flex hip and knee, then extend knee — pain/resistance is positive. Brudzinski's sign: passive neck flexion causes hip/knee flexion. Both indicate meningeal irritation. B, C are incorrect descriptions. D is incorrect — these signs are used in children.

questions.question: A child is having a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. What is the priority nursing intervention?
options: Restrain the child to prevent injury
Place the child in a supine position
Clear the area of hazards and protect the head
Insert a tongue blade between teeth
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Protect the child during seizure: clear area of hazards, protect head, loosen tight clothing, position on side after seizure. A is incorrect — restraint can cause injury. B is incorrect — position on side after seizure. D is incorrect — never insert objects in mouth (aspiration risk).

questions.question: Which signs and symptoms are characteristic of hydrocephalus? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — head circumference increases. B is correct — fontanelle bulges. C is correct — setting sun sign. D is correct — high-pitched cry. E is incorrect — suture lines widen in hydrocephalus, not narrow.

questions.question: What is the most common type of cerebral palsy?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Spastic cerebral palsy is the most common type (~70-80%). It is characterized by increased muscle tone, hyperreflexia, and scissor gait. A is incorrect — dyskinetic is ~10-20%. B is incorrect — ataxic is ~5-10%. D is incorrect — mixed is combination.

questions.question: Match each neurological disorder with its key feature:
rows: Sudden onset of fever, headache, stiff neck, positive Kernig's sign
Abnormal head growth with bulging fontanelle
Involuntary movements and difficulty with coordination
questions.rationale: Correct: Meningitis (fever, stiff neck, Kernig's) → A. Epilepsy (repeated seizures) → B. Hydrocephalus (abnormal head growth, bulging fontanelle) → C. Cerebral Palsy (involuntary movements, coordination difficulty) → D. This tests key features of common pediatric neurological disorders.

questions.question: A parent notices their 4-year-old's eyes appear to cross intermittently. What is the priority teaching?
options: This is normal in preschoolers and will resolve
Covering the stronger eye is not an effective treatment
Early treatment is important to prevent amblyopia
Surgery is always the first-line treatment
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Early treatment of strabismus is critical to prevent amblyopia (lazy eye). Treatment includes patching the stronger eye, glasses, or surgery. A is incorrect — intermittent strabismus after 6 months requires evaluation. B is incorrect — patching is effective. D is incorrect — patching/glasses often tried first.

questions.question: Which statements about Reye syndrome are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Reye syndrome is associated with aspirin use during viral illness.
It causes acute encephalopathy and fatty liver.
It is most common in children under 15 years.
Acetaminophen is a safe alternative to aspirin.
It is a common childhood illness.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — aspirin + viral illness (flu, chickenpox) triggers Reye. B is correct — encephalopathy and fatty liver infiltration. C is correct — under 15 years. D is correct — acetaminophen is safe alternative. E is incorrect — Reye is rare, especially with aspirin warnings.

questions.question: The Adams forward bend test is used to screen for:
questions.rationale: Correct (B): The Adams forward bend test screens for scoliosis. The child bends forward — asymmetry in the back (rib hump) indicates possible scoliosis. A is incorrect — clubfoot is foot deformity. C is incorrect — DDH uses Ortolani/Barlow tests. D is incorrect — CP is neurological.

questions.question: Which test is used to screen for developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH) in infants?
questions.rationale: Correct (A): Barlow test: adduction with posterior pressure to check for dislocation. Ortolani test: abduction with anterior pressure to check for reduction. A click or clunk is positive. B is incorrect — for scoliosis. C is incorrect — for Duchenne muscular dystrophy. D is incorrect — for neurological testing.

questions.question: Which are the 5 P's of neurovascular assessment in a child with a fracture? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D, E): All five are correct. Pain (out of proportion), Pallor (poor circulation), Pulse (absent or weak), Paresthesia (numbness/tingling), Paralysis (loss of movement). These assess for compartment syndrome or neurovascular compromise.

questions.question: A child is placed in Buck's traction for a femur fracture. What is the priority nursing intervention?
options: Allow the child to move freely in bed
Keep the bed flat with no elevation
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Frequent neurovascular checks (5 P's) are critical with any traction to detect compartment syndrome or nerve/vascular compromise. A is incorrect — child should be positioned correctly. C is incorrect — weights should never be removed. D is incorrect — bed may be elevated for counter-traction.

questions.question: A boy with suspected Duchenne muscular dystrophy demonstrates Gower's sign. What is this?
options: Using hands to 'walk up' the legs when rising from the floor
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Gower's sign is when a child uses hands to push off knees and 'walk up' the legs when rising from sitting or squatting. It indicates proximal muscle weakness. A is incorrect — toe walking may occur but not Gower's. C is incorrect — not the sign. D is incorrect — scissor gait is spastic CP.

questions.question: A 14-year-old reports feeling sad most days, loss of interest in activities, and difficulty sleeping. What is the most appropriate screening tool?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) is a validated depression screening tool for adolescents and adults. A is incorrect — GCS for neurological status. C is incorrect — APGAR for newborn. D is incorrect — Denver II for developmental screening.

questions.question: Which statements about adolescent eating disorders are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Anorexia nervosa involves restriction of food intake and fear of gaining weight.
Bulimia nervosa involves binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors.
Binge eating disorder involves recurrent binge eating without compensatory behaviors.
Eating disorders are more common in boys than girls.
Medical complications include electrolyte imbalances and cardiac arrhythmias.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, E): A is correct — anorexia is restriction and fear of weight gain. B is correct — bulimia is binge + purge/compensation. C is correct — binge eating disorder has no compensation. E is correct — medical complications are serious. D is incorrect — more common in girls.

questions.question: What is the primary treatment for clubfoot (talipes equinovarus) in infants?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): The Ponseti method involves serial casting to gradually correct the deformity, followed by bracing (Denis Browne bar). Surgery is reserved for resistant cases. A is incorrect — surgery is not first-line. C is incorrect — casting is primary. D is incorrect — early treatment is essential.

questions.question: A 16-year-old presents with weight loss, irregular periods, and excessive exercise. Identify the primary concern and select the most appropriate intervention.
options: Concern: Anorexia nervosa with risk of electrolyte imbalance
Intervention: Tell the teen to eat more
Intervention: Recommend more exercise to build strength
questions.rationale: Correct (A and D): The symptoms (weight loss, irregular periods, excessive exercise) suggest anorexia nervosa. Immediate hospitalization for stabilization (electrolytes, cardiac monitoring) is critical. B is incorrect — this is not normal growth. C is incorrect — not hyperthyroidism. E is incorrect — dismissive. F is incorrect — dangerous advice.

questions.question: What is the primary treatment for amblyopia (lazy eye) in a child?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Patching the stronger eye forces the weaker eye to work harder, improving vision. Treatment is most effective before age 7-8. A is incorrect — surgery may be for strabismus, not amblyopia. C is incorrect — drops may be used but patching is primary. D is incorrect — amblyopia requires treatment.

questions.question: Which statements about preparing children for hospitalization are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Toddlers need simple explanations just before procedures.
School-age children can handle equipment and ask questions.
Adolescents benefit from involvement in decisions.
Preschoolers should be prepared days in advance.
All children should be told 'it won't hurt'.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C): A is correct — toddlers: simple, just before. B is correct — school-age: touch equipment. C is correct — adolescents: involvement. D is incorrect — days in advance causes anxiety in preschoolers. E is incorrect — never lie; be honest about sensations.

questions.question: Match each musculoskeletal disorder with its key test or sign:
questions.rationale: Correct: DDH (Ortolani/Barlow) → A. Scoliosis (Adams forward bend) → B. Duchenne (Gower's sign) → C. Clubfoot (Ponseti method) → D. This tests key assessment/treatment for common musculoskeletal disorders.

questions.question: A 17-year-old refuses to follow the treatment plan and argues with parents about appointments. This is an example of:
options: Normal development of autonomy and identity
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Adolescents strive for autonomy and identity. Questioning authority and treatment plans is normal developmental behavior. A is incorrect — not abnormal. C is incorrect — not depression. D is incorrect — not parenting issue.

questions.question: Which are appropriate seizure precautions for a hospitalized child? (Select all that apply.)
options: Have suction and oxygen at bedside
Stay with the child during seizure
Insert tongue blade if tongue is bitten
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, D): A is correct — pad rails. B is correct — suction and oxygen ready. D is correct — stay with child. C is incorrect — never restrain (causes injury). E is incorrect — never insert objects in mouth.

questions.question: A 13-year-old with scoliosis has a 28-degree curve. What is the likely treatment?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Curves 25-45 degrees in growing adolescents are treated with bracing to prevent progression. Surgery is for curves >45-50 degrees. A is incorrect — 28 degrees requires treatment. C is incorrect — surgery for larger curves. D is incorrect — bracing is primary.

questions.question: A child with cerebral palsy has increased muscle tone and scissor gait. This is _____ cerebral palsy, the most common type.
questions.rationale: Correct (A): Spastic cerebral palsy is the most common type (~70-80%). It is characterized by increased muscle tone (hypertonia), hyperreflexia, and scissor gait (legs cross when walking).

questions.question: At what age is the first dose of meningococcal conjugate vaccine (MenACWY) recommended?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): MenACWY is recommended at 11-12 years with a booster at 16 years. A is incorrect — not at 6 months. B is incorrect — not at 2 years. D is incorrect — 16 years is the booster.

questions.question: Which statements about the Pavlik harness are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It is used to treat developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH).
It maintains the hips in flexion and abduction.
It is typically worn 23 hours per day.
It is used for clubfoot correction.
It should be removed for bathing.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C): A is correct — Pavlik harness treats DDH. B is correct — maintains flexion and abduction. C is correct — worn 23 hours/day. D is incorrect — for clubfoot is Ponseti method. E is incorrect — typically left on for bathing.

questions.question: A parent is teaching about adolescent safety. Highlight the THREE correct statements.
options: Motor vehicle accidents are the leading cause of death in adolescents.
Texting while driving is safe if the teen is experienced.
Suicide is the second leading cause of death in adolescents.
Adolescents should never wear seat belts.
The HEADSS assessment screens for psychosocial risks.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, C, E): MVA is leading cause of death (A), suicide is second (C), HEADSS screens psychosocial risks (E). B is incorrect — texting is dangerous. D is incorrect — seat belts are essential.

questions.question: What is the recommended age for the first dose of human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): HPV vaccine is recommended at 11-12 years (can be given as early as 9). Two doses if started before age 15; three doses if started at 15+. A is incorrect — can be given but not the standard recommendation. C and D are incorrect — too late for optimal protection.

questions.question: Which assessments are part of a neurological check for a child with a head injury? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — LOC is critical. B is correct — pupils indicate brain function. C is correct — motor assessment. D is correct — vital signs. E is incorrect — not part of neuro check (though altered sleep may be noted).

questions.question: In boys, which occurs first during puberty?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Testicular growth is the first sign of puberty in boys (Tanner stage 2). This precedes pubic hair and penile growth. A is incorrect — pubic hair follows. B is incorrect — follows testicular growth. D is incorrect — axillary hair is later.

questions.question: The Ponseti method for clubfoot correction involves:
questions.rationale: Correct (B): The Ponseti method uses serial casting (weekly) to gradually correct the deformity, followed by bracing (Denis Browne bar) to maintain correction. Surgery is reserved for resistant cases. A is incorrect — surgery not first. C is incorrect — casting is primary. D is incorrect — early treatment essential.

questions.question: A 15-year-old presents with weight loss, amenorrhea, and excessive exercise. Which findings support anorexia nervosa? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, E): A is correct — low BMI. B is correct — fear of weight gain. C is correct — distorted body image. E is correct — electrolyte issues. D is incorrect — binge eating is bulimia/binge eating disorder.

questions.question: A child has brief staring spells lasting 10-20 seconds with no postictal confusion. What type of seizure is this?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Absence seizures (petit mal) are brief staring spells (5-20 seconds) with abrupt onset and no postictal confusion. Common in children 4-10 years. A is incorrect — tonic-clonic has convulsions. C is incorrect — partial complex has automatisms. D is incorrect — related to fever.

questions.question: A 2-month-old is diagnosed with developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH). What is the primary treatment?
questions.rationale: Correct (A): The Pavlik harness is the primary treatment for DDH in infants <6 months. It maintains hips in flexion and abduction. Surgery is for older infants or failed harness. A is correct — Pavlik harness.

questions.question: Which are important nursing interventions for a child in skeletal traction? (Select all that apply.)
options: Never remove weights without provider order
Perform pin site care as ordered
Allow child to move freely in bed
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — frequent neuro checks. B is correct — never remove weights. C is correct — weights hang freely. D is correct — pin care. E is incorrect — child should be positioned correctly, not move freely.

questions.question: What is the recommended amount of sleep for adolescents?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Adolescents need 8-10 hours of sleep per night. Sleep deprivation affects mood, concentration, and school performance. A is incorrect — too little. B is incorrect — borderline. D is incorrect — too much.

questions.question: Match each vaccine with the correct recommendation:
questions.rationale: Correct: MenACWY (11-12 + 16) → A. HPV (11-12, 2-3 doses) → B. Tdap (11-12) → C. Flu (annually) → D. These are the standard adolescent vaccine recommendations.

questions.question: A child with cerebral palsy has involuntary movements and difficulty controlling posture. This is:
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Dyskinetic (athetoid) CP involves involuntary, writhing movements and difficulty with posture control. It is caused by damage to the basal ganglia. A is incorrect — spastic is increased tone. C is incorrect — ataxic is balance/coordination issues. D is incorrect — not mixed.

questions.question: Which assessments are important for a child with a musculoskeletal disorder? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D, E): All are important. Neurovascular (5 P's) for circulation/nerve. ROM and strength for function. Pain for comfort. Gait/posture for alignment and mobility.

questions.question: A 14-year-old with a femur fracture in traction reports severe pain, pallor, and tingling in the foot. Identify the concern and select the priority intervention.
questions.rationale: Correct (A and D): Severe pain, pallor, and paresthesia (tingling) are signs of compartment syndrome — a medical emergency. Notify provider immediately for possible fasciotomy. B is incorrect — this is not normal pain. C is incorrect — not the cause. E is incorrect — pain med won't fix compartment syndrome. F is incorrect — elevation may worsen.

questions.question: During a HEADSS assessment, an adolescent admits to occasional alcohol use. What is the next step?
options: Ask about frequency, quantity, and context
Ignore it as normal adolescent behavior
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Explore further — ask about frequency, quantity, context, and other substance use. A is incorrect — not mandatory reporting unless safety concern. C is incorrect — not first step. D is incorrect — needs exploration.

questions.question: At what curve degree is surgery typically recommended for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Surgery is typically recommended for curves >45-50 degrees to prevent progression and respiratory compromise. A is incorrect — observation or bracing. B is incorrect — bracing. D is incorrect — too severe.

questions.question: Which statements about seizure safety teaching are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Turn child on side after seizure ends.
Place something soft under the head.
Call 911 if seizure lasts more than 5 minutes.
Insert a tongue blade during seizure.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — side-lying prevents aspiration. B is correct — never restrain. C is correct — protect head. D is correct — status epilepticus. E is incorrect — never insert objects in mouth.

questions.question: A 16-year-old reports feeling sad, hopeless, and has lost interest in friends and activities. This is a sign of _____, which is the _____ leading cause of death in adolescents.
questions.rationale: Correct: Depression (A) and second (C). Depression symptoms include sadness, hopelessness, loss of interest. Suicide (often related to depression) is the second leading cause of death in adolescents after motor vehicle accidents.

questions.question: A parent asks about the Pavlik harness for their infant with DDH. What is correct teaching?
options: Remove the harness for 4 hours daily
Keep the harness on 23 hours per day
It should be worn only at night
Bathing is done with harness off
questions.rationale: Correct (B): The Pavlik harness is worn 23 hours per day for effective treatment. It maintains hips in flexion and abduction. Removal is only for bathing if provider allows. A, C, D are incorrect — keep on as prescribed.

questions.question: Which statements about adolescent nutrition are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Calcium intake is critical for bone development.
Iron needs increase due to growth and menstruation.
Adolescents need more calories than adults.
Skipping meals is healthy for weight control.
Calcium and vitamin D prevent osteoporosis later.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, E): A is correct — calcium for bone. B is correct — iron needs increase. C is correct — higher calorie needs. E is correct — prevention. D is incorrect — skipping meals is unhealthy.

questions.question: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is:
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Duchenne is a progressive, fatal X-linked disorder caused by dystrophin gene mutation. It primarily affects boys. There is no cure, though corticosteroids can slow progression. A is incorrect — not curable. C is incorrect — boys. D is incorrect — genetic.

questions.question: A nurse is teaching about meningitis. Highlight the THREE correct signs/symptoms.
options: Stiff neck and positive Kernig's sign
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, D): Sudden fever (A), stiff neck/Kernig's (B), headache/photophobia (D). C is incorrect — petechial rash (meningococcal) does NOT blanch. E is incorrect — not just runny nose.

questions.question: Match each condition with its primary sign:
questions.rationale: Correct: Scoliosis (rib hump) → A. DDH (Ortolani positive) → B. Clubfoot (inward foot) → C. Duchenne (Gower's) → D.

questions.question: Which interventions reduce hospitalization stress for a school-age child? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — hands-on. B is correct — routines. C is correct — detailed explanation. D is correct — activities. E is incorrect — parent presence reduces stress.

questions.question: What is the most effective intervention to reduce adolescent motor vehicle deaths?
questions.rationale: Correct (D): All are effective. Graduated licensing (A), cell phone restrictions (B), and defensive driving education (C) all reduce MVA deaths. Comprehensive approaches are most effective.

questions.question: A 12-year-old with a femur fracture reports tingling, numbness, and severe pain in the calf. Identify the concern and priority intervention.
questions.rationale: Correct (A and D): Tingling, numbness, and severe pain in the calf indicate compartment syndrome — a surgical emergency. Notify provider immediately. B is incorrect — not healing. C is incorrect — not infection. E is incorrect — ice/elevation may worsen. F is incorrect — pain med won't fix compartment syndrome.

questions.question: Which medications are commonly used for seizure management in children? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — phenytoin. B is correct — carbamazepine. C is correct — valproic acid. D is correct — levetiracetam. E is incorrect — ibuprofen is analgesic, not antiepileptic.

questions.question: A child with hydrocephalus has a VP shunt. A sudden increase in _____ circumference, _____ fontanelle, and _____ cry indicates shunt malfunction.
questions.rationale: Correct: Head circumference (A), bulging fontanelle (B), high-pitched cry (C). These are signs of increased ICP from shunt malfunction. Abdomen (D) is not a sign of shunt malfunction.

questions.question: Which screening should be performed at every adolescent well visit?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Depression screening (e.g., PHQ-9) should be performed at every adolescent well visit. Suicide is the second leading cause of death in adolescents. A is incorrect — not for teens. C is incorrect — not for teens. D is incorrect — not routine.

questions.question: Which are appropriate interventions for a child having a seizure? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — clear hazards. B is correct — protect head. C is correct — time seizure. D is correct — side after. E is incorrect — never restrain.

questions.question: Which age group has the highest risk for separation anxiety during hospitalization?
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Toddlers (1-3 years) have the highest risk for separation anxiety. They have strong attachment to parents and limited understanding of time. A is incorrect — infants show distress but less separation anxiety. C is incorrect — preschoolers have more understanding. D is incorrect — school-age has coping skills.

questions.question: Which statements about NGN question types are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Matrix questions require matching rows to columns.
Bowtie questions assess cause-effect and interventions.
SATA questions have only one correct answer.
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — matrix matches rows to columns. B is correct — bowtie assesses cause-effect and interventions. C is correct — cloze is drag-and-drop. D is correct — highlight selects text. E is incorrect — SATA can have multiple correct answers.

questions.question: Match each intervention with the correct condition:
questions.rationale: Correct: Pavlik (DDH) → A. Ponseti (clubfoot) → B. Boston brace (scoliosis) → C. Gower's (Duchenne) → D.

questions.question: Which vaccine should be given to all adolescents at 11-12 years?
questions.rationale: Correct (C): Tdap (tetanus, diphtheria, pertussis) is given at 11-12 years. Rotavirus (A) is for infants. Pneumococcal (B) is for infants/elderly. Varicella (D) is given at 12 months and 4-6 years.

questions.question: Which are signs of hydrocephalus in an infant? (Select all that apply.)
questions.rationale: Correct (A, B, C, D): A is correct — increasing head size. B is correct — bulging fontanelle. C is correct — setting sun sign. D is correct — high-pitched cry. E is incorrect — suture lines widen.

questions.question: A 16-year-old is experiencing identity confusion, experimenting with different roles, and questioning values. This is consistent with:
questions.rationale: Correct (B): Identity vs Role Confusion (12-18 years) involves questioning values, experimenting with roles, and developing a sense of self. A is incorrect — Industry is school-age. C is incorrect — Concrete Operational is cognitive, not psychosocial. D is incorrect — Postconventional is moral reasoning.
